author: @HelenmarieCancellier-ob4pv
text:
We really need to start calling mental illness. Brain illness because it is a brain illness, people need to stop putting. Their Negative thoughts and feelings about mental.illness to themselves Depression is a serious thing. I face it everyday.
 And what discussed me the most in this world? Is the people that do not understand it? That thinks it can go away with authorities. And it can't it feel with a heart disease? It would be understood if it was a liver disease. It would be understood.  And it would be accepted and it would be helped without condemnation and judgment. On the person suffering it, people need to wake up.
 I didn't know of this man until I saw this post but I feel for him and I feel For his family. Please, if you're out there and you feel like you need help reach out to somebody.There are people out there that will listen And if you can't find anybody find a way to get in contact with me and I won't listen because I know people are important. Every person is important that reportson should be felt loand I'm sure he did feel love from his family.
 But a lot of other people don't.  And if you're one of those people that don't feel loved or need it or want to know that you are you are important? You are loved you are needed.
 This video hit me really, really hard and I hope everybody out. There's a good positive attitude today. Like I said, if you can't reach out to somebody else you can reach out to me. I'm a good listener.  And I understand Just know whoever is reading this. That needs to see it knew that you are loved wanted and needed. Don't let the thoughts in your head overtake you And believe me, I know it's easier said than done reach out for help.If you need it please😢❤

author: @donparkinson9561
text: Thank you Dave, for sharing this message with us. I loved watching all his videos and found them to all be interesting. I hope his site will remain up on YouTube for a while longer. I know how important it is when we lose our spouse. The vacuum is unbearable. Fortunately, in my case, a friend asked me if I would be interested in seeing a friend of hers. I didn't know what to do with myself, so I accepted. It saved me from an alternate timeline. I am now remarried. I like that you shared the photograph of Rob & Patti. Patricia was my late wife's name also. Because their love was so great, there is the distinct possibility that they will reincarnate and be a couple once more. I have known couples that have done this for centuries. Within your own family lineage, remember their traits. See if those traits manifest in any of your children or grandchildren.

author: @michaelsohocki1573
text:
I'm heartbroken. Robert Murray Smith was a father to thinkers everywhere. Inspiration translates literally "to breathe life into": every slide of his recorded work contains it.

PLEASE DON'T LET HIS WORK DIE.

Rob was the Benjamin Franklin of our time. His collected work must be gathered in a box set in hard copy, sold far and wide, handed to curious children and adults everywhere, people at the beginnings of their journeys, not sure what is possible, or reasons to do them--Rob's work gave us direction not just in how to do things but how to think about them, the whole
universe. Few people ever deliver what really changes how people see. Rob did.

It should be distributed to every college and library in the galaxy.

author: @mikefriend1134
text:
David, thank you for posting this, and letting us know what happened to Rob. I can't imagine what you're feeling, but I do feel certain that you did everything you were able to support him. It's clear that you loved him deeply, and I hope you're able to feel how much you did to help him.

I think you've chosen a fantastic way to memorialize him, honoring his memory by easing suffering.

 I'm saddened by his death, and the manner of it, and how much pain he was in. Even so, his memory will be a source of hope and inspiration for me, just as he was in his life, just as he was for so many people.

I hope you and your family have the support you need in such a painful moment, and that you won't hesitate to ask for any help you need. Take care of yourselves, and let others take care of you!

Here's to you, Rob.

author: @JPBroadcasting
text: Robert literally saved my life... i was homeless for 3 years due to a bad economy in my city, i was working full time and still couldn't afford rent. I have been a fan for a few years prior to that. I built about 4 different heaters based on Robs designs... the cakepan and tin can radiant heater kept me from freezing to death in -35c temps during 3 canadian winters. If it wasn't for Rob, i wouldn't be here, and i could never thank him enough. Im going to light that heater up one last time tonight. Rest in peace, my favorite and easily the best science teacher!

author: @brawndo8726
text:
I admired Robert's work so much and could never understand why he wasn't using his genius to produce world changing products. But I chalked it up to the fact that he had so much to teach the world that investing time in any one thing would limit what he was able to share with others who might take up that challenge themselves. 

RIP Robert. I pray that you accomplished everything you intended in this world.

author: @MiracleManM
text:
wow. so sad for you and your family.  The universe has lost another good one.  I lost my best friend through suicide 2022, and it appeared to be out of the blue - or at least until you start to look back at some of  the signs.  I lost my mother in 2012 and my Father has really struggled since.  For someone like him (very proud ex-RN Commodore) to hear him mention on a few occasions he came close to ending it has been hard to hear and deal with tbh.   Suicide is the biggest killer of men under 50 - not cancer, not accidents, but suicide. Just think about that for a minute.  We don't deal with loss or failure well.  Ive always been the type to be quite flippant of people 'not quite hacking life'.  But going through this gave me a new perspective.  

I had the opportunity to become a mental health first aider something I scoffed at tbh when I first heard the term, but I had a thought - what if It meant I would have spotted the signs - or been able to say something that may have helped in the days before?  That for me was enough.  Actually it was useful, and while I don't think I'll ever be a very 'good' one.  I realised that even if there was a 5% greater chance in say that one thing that might sway someone away from doing something they cant come back from - that was enough.  Godspeed Robert.

author: @kneesovertoes
text: Dave, I’m so sorry for your family’s loss. I came across your brother’s videos here a few years ago—like a breath of fresh air. At a time when even educational content can feel commercial or exploitative, Robert’s channel was a refuge of authenticity. The curiosity, enthusiasm, wit, and childlike love he brought to his work changed my life in beautiful and unexpected ways. I always hoped I’d have the chance to meet him one day and pick his brain. It’s difficult to think how much pain he must have been in, even while continuing to create such impassioned and inspiring work. There are no words that can lessen the blow of losing such a beloved person; my hope is that his work and his memory continue to inspire the hearts and minds of creative thinkers the world over for many years to come. ❤ 🙏 10/09/2025

author: @alden1132
text:
I'm devastated. If the condolences of a stranger in the internet can mean anything, know, you have mine.
     I got a terrible feeling whenddcž he posted that video, which has persisted up to the very moment I found this video. His last video felt just like a irrevocable farewell to we, his fans and admirers, and it's unpleasant to learn it was was.
     I'm sorry to say that I can personally relate to the pain of losing a brother to suicide. I know well the myriad emotions such an event can engender, so I hope you believe me when I say that I truly, deeply commiserate.
     At the same time, I cannot find it in my heart to condemn or resent Robert for making this decision, even if I wish I could change it. Still, pain is among the most personal things humans can experience, and only the one experiencing it can truly judge how unbearable it is. I believe its important to say that, as painful as this situatinon is for those he left behind, we shouldn't judge him harshly for his decision, because, as sapient creatures, the right and ability to refuse acknowledge is about as  is, I really, deeply appreciate your taking the time to record this video, and explain the harsh, yet irrefutable truths of the situation.
Good luck, in dealing with the next week's and months ahead. Know you're not alone.

author: @arebuffat
text:
It is with profound sorrow that I learned of Robert’s passing, and my heart goes out to his family and his closest friends. I am only one among his 570,000 subscribers, yet like so many others, I was deeply moved by his scientific rigor and the way he transformed hard-won knowledge into practical guidance for all of us.
As I write these lines, grief sits heavy. I will never presume to understand his pain, but I recognize the depth of suffering that can lead to such a decision. What endures is the good he gave. His enthusiasm, his creative spirit, and his straightforward, disciplined approach to science opened doors and lit the path for countless people. His work, alongside that of Larry Hall, carried me through some of the hardest seasons of my life. It sparked the courage to test my abilities and to take concrete actions for the well-being of those I love.
Robert’s legacy lives in every experiment we attempt, every question we dare to ask, and every humble act of building and learning that his videos inspired. May we honor him by seeking understanding with kindness, sharing what we learn, and caring for one another. To his family and friends, please accept my deepest respect and condolences. May his memory be a blessing and a source of strength.
Oui, Robert, tu vas nous manquer terriblement, et oui, je ne t’oublierai jamais.

author: @marklagana2769
text:
thankyou and your family for acting with such grace in this time, we should all aspire to communicate as candidly because as mentioned we could read between the lines of Roberts carefully chosen words to know that something is not right.

I take comfort in knowing he passed comfortably. He will always be an inspiration in the amazing STEM educational videos the man pumped out pouring his heart, sense of humour, intelligence and creativity into.

I was saddened by his language in his last video. We will all face things beyond our control, the key is to not live miserably in solitude. We need social interaction and community. People live with horrible pain from birth yet are still able to find joy and happiness.

People experiencing grief and pain are statistically more likely to recover and life a meaningful life with the help of a trained pyschologist. I was very stubborn in not seeing a pain pyschologist, men have it engrained into us to project a stoic image of strength.

if you're struggling, there is hope - talk to a professional (therapist/pyschologist). It might not be a quick fix but you won't regret it

author: @danielroibert5631
text:
Hello,

I sympathize with your ordeal and I am sorry to learn that Mr. Murray was suffering from such pain. However, I find it inappropriate to publish this kind of video. Indeed, Mr. Murray’s audience may have been wider than you seem to realize. There are children and teenagers passionate about mechanics and 3D printing who will see this video without being prepared for it. They will hear you say that there can be several good reasons to commit suicide. This is unacceptable without proper guidance, and you cannot ensure that all vulnerable viewers are adequately supported.

It seems to me that you should add a clear disclaimer and correct the idea that there could be good reasons to commit suicide. Even if such reasons might exist, they should not be expressed in that manner.

If the human values you seem to wish to share are truly your own, I hope you will understand my concern and my strong reservations regarding some of your statements and the platform you chose to share your feelings with people who are neither prepared nor necessarily capable of coping with such content, and who may in fact be too fragile.

replies.author: @dandycat2204
replies.text:
Well, the thumbnail makes it clear that Mr Murray-Smith has passed, and that this video was to discuss the circumstances behind that passing.

The explanation for the way he chose the path he did was dealt with sensitively and honestly.

Children and teenagers should not be spared the notion of death, it is a fact of life, and one that we will all face eventually.

I found the video to be moving, and personal.

A fitting tribute to the man himself.

replies.author: @danielroibert5631
replies.text:
@dandycat2204  I agree that children and teenagers should not be kept away from such topics, but they need proper guidance when these subjects are addressed.
Here, you take no precautions to ensure that vulnerable people are in a suitable state to face this. You bring up the subject without considering that unprepared viewers may come across it.
Let me remind you that the usual theme of this channel has nothing to do with such matters. When a channel has a clear identity, it should not suddenly shift its tone or content without warning.

The topic of death is one thing, but here we are talking about suicide. I hope you are aware that suicide has become a serious concern among young people nowadays. If you don’t take care to approach this issue within a guided or supportive context, teenagers may end up seeing it as something normal. It is vital to make it clear that suicide is not a normal act — and you fail to take this basic precaution here.

author: @iExistOne
text:
I am sad to hear the news about his passing. Robert Murray-Smith inspired me, I bought a 3D printer because of his content about them.
His perspective was insightful. Sometimes he was crass in his view, but I liked his perspective because it caused me to think through ideas. Being a scientist, he was thick skinned to criticism and offered counter points for explanations.
I did notice his mentality changed in more recent content, close to the end he gave up on planned content, the solar panel build video as an example. His toleration of criticism was becoming limited and he would respond carelessly. I thought something was odd, but I wasn't sure what it was. I said so in his motor series. He promoted designs but didn't really test any load capacities, running a diode isn't the same as charging a cell phone. It's like he was only doing half of the work he usually did, which isn't like his older content where he explored a design more thoroughly.
Jeremiah 29
“The blessing of a perishing man came upon me, and I caused the widow’s heart to sing for joy.”

author: @ICGedye
text:
Oh my David, my heartfelt feelings go out to you and Robert’s family. Thank you for making this video and updating us during what must be excruciating tough to take. You paid him a wonderful tribute, I just wished I hadn’t seen it - I’m numb and in tears. 
I’m only nine years younger, and I’m facing my own mental health issues at the moment following recent damage to my better hand and affecting my manual job. To me Rob was a breath of fresh air blending old school techniques and ways of delivering ‘lectures’ on engineering principles with new age tech and modern application. I was lucky enough to receive my toolroom training during the late 80’s working with characters like Rob before there was a decline in process and production engineering basically throughout the South. His channel brought that stuff back to the mainstream and I will miss him greatly. I knew he must have found the loss of Patti very tough to carry on with, which made his continuing output all the more remarkable. He did a great job, but there was an inexplicable sense of finality in his last video. A query in my head now answered. I wish you well sir, and thanks for bringing the charity Calm to my attention, I will be donating. Than you once more David. May Robert rest in peace.

author: @308dad8
text: Man I’m so sorry to hear it. He was always so happy and excited about all his projects. Whether passive wind harvesting or various types of batteries and homemade batteries. He made a battery in the yard or park.  His videos were a distraction through some of my least motivated moments. He made me want to get a project finished and off my bench. I’m terrible about starting them and never finishing them. I got to remember what it was like when I could stay focused by watching him. I’m terribly sorry he was in such misery all that time. I wish he was still with us. I know you’re hurting even more knowing him personally being his brother. My warmest condolences to you and the rest of his family. I’m terribly sorry for this tragic loss. I wasn’t particularly interested in all his projects but he was a brilliant man, making homemade batteries and capacitors for fun and he was so happy I rarely didn’t finish a video I started to watch of his. I wish he knew how much good he put into the world with his coloring his ideas. He made how many almost free space heaters to show us how to make safe heat that’s nearly free to operate. I’ll pray for you to receive comfort from the Holy Spirit.

Thank you very much for telling us.  I think I speak for all of us when I say that we send our love to Robert and you and the family.  


I knew, deep down, when I saw his last video, that it really was a goodbye, even if I didn't want to believe it.

Robert had his YouTube channel set up with memberships, and content behind it.  I am curious if the money from those memberships will be going towards this charity or the family.  I don't particularly need to know which, only that it's going to go somewhere other than youtube's pocket.  
If not, is it possible to remove the paywalls from the content Rob put out so that we can all benefit from it?  

He really was a true genius of our time.  Choosing to share his passion, and teach along the way, being an inspiration to us all.  He will be greatly missed.

author: @Mithrral
text: I am absolutely devastated.  Robert's channel helped me make it through some of the darkest times of my life when I didn't know if I had the strength to keep going on.  At my lowest point, this man sharing videos of him tinkering and creating inventions solely for the betterment of mankind and the joy in his laughter when testing a new device gave me the strength to pick myself back up and rebuild my life.  I bought notebooks and started scribbling inventions and thinking about energy generation.  It became my go-to method of therapy for dealing with the crushing stress and depression I was going through.  This man was my Bob Ross.  I am doing very well for myself now.  I am physically, emotionally, and financially in a much better place, and I honestly do not know if I would even be here today without Robert and his videos.  Please leave this channel up, if only for the chance that someone like me will eventually be inspired to keep going through his legacy.  I can only strive to be as kind and good in life as Robert.  My best wishes and prayers to his family members, and may his soul be at peace.

author: @ThatRobHuman
text:
I'm so sorry to hear of this, and my heart goes out to you and your family, David - and to the hundreds of thousands of people who he's entertained and taught over the years.

For what it is worth, David, I hope you take your time on making a memorial video - I cannot imagine how hard it will be to comb through so much footage so soon and I would hate to see you unduly inflict that upon yourself out of some sense of obligation - please be sure to take care of yourself, too; we will wait as long as you need - and if it turns out you can't bring yourself to do it: that's more than alright too.  I feel like the channel itself stands as a small virtual cathedral to education in it's own right. In true Robert fashion: he ended up building his own memorial himself.

author: @longevityonabudget
text:
Hi Dave. I am so terribly sorry for your loss. It must be very hard for you and your family and I sincerely wish you great comfort and great memories.

Your brother was simply a natural genius. He could make any thing as if he was an expert at each project he attempted, and was of the first YT creators i followed regularly.

I have also lost a sibling to suicide and it took a lot of working out. In the end i simply have to accept that a choice was made and choose to remember the good times.

I certainly will make a donation in his memory. He certainly provided me with a lot of value and entertainment, so it's the least i can do.

I echo the others here, and encourage you to consider leaving this channel up. I will not judge you, however, for whatever decision you make.

Rest in Peace, Robert Murray Smith.

author: @Fastfreddyii
text:
I'm not a regular channel watcher, but I did take great interest in the 'wind energy generator' project. I very much liked Roberts research, and the presentation. I'm saddened at his passing. 

Some 20 years ago I lost a family friend I had known since his birth, who had to give up his struggle for a similar reason. He was a keen horse rider, and took a fall that produced a bad head injury. He survived, but was plagued by migraines afterwards. Many people fortunately, don't get to experience long term severe pain. I did once for three months, with an undiagnosed rotting nerve in a tooth, triggering trigeminal neuralgia. Even strong prescription strength medication would not touch it. (Normal pain medication doesn't work on nerves.) It felt like I was having a nail hammered into my jaw, for several hours a day. Thankfully it wasn't constant for those three months. I was fairly certain, it was unlikely I could survive for 12 months with the condition. I saw 3 dentists, and two doctors without a correct diagnosis. Finally, a Nurse Practitioner, worked out the problem, and my condition was resolved within days. I can't explain the feeling of relief.

My family friend wasn't so lucky. He endured several years without longterm respite. If you haven't experienced a migraine so bad it makes you physically sick with the pain, imagine breaking your arm most days of the week, or giving birth, most days of the week. Not only enduring the 'at the moment' pain, but knowing that tomorrow, there was more to come. If not tomorrow then certainly the day after. 

 I've made a small donation to CALM in Roberts name. My condolences to his family, and his many friends.

author: @caiteallam3017
text:
Thank you Dave for that most heartfelt announcement; as difficult as it was to hear, it was helpful to hear it from you and your genuine approach provided some closure and solice.  My condolonces to you, his family and loved ones, and to all on this channel and abroad (myself included) who have been touched by his life and light.

May God share His graces generously with Robert as Robert so generously shared his talents with us🙏🏼  God have mercy,  may Robert find the peace and well being he so deserves and may his union Patty be blessed.

He had a go of it. He lived his best life. He shared his heart and talents with the world.  Please keep this channel up, his content is pure genious, and I refer to his videos often. 

I was so saddened to hear when Patty died, We could tell how much she was for him, and I could sense he wanted to stay near to her.  Rob put his best foot foward and would never let you know the depth of his pain.  One way or another God has a plan for all of us, and while Robert is resting now, I hope his light will continue to shine forth 🙏🏼

author: @robnic52
text: I won't forget Rob. I remember how shocked and sad I was when he announced that Patti had died. He showed short videos of them both presenting together, she was as engaging, natural and enthusiastic as Rob on camera. Like two peas in a pod, I realised then that he would have a hard time without her. But Rob retained such a professional and calm public persona afterwards that I never imagined he might resort to suicide. It's so often the ones you'd never guess are struggling. Rob and Patti, two lives well lived.

text: Dear Dave, Thank you so very much for recording and posting this video. It has made me tremendously sad, and I send you and your family, as well as all Robert’s friends, my truly deepest condolences. My brother took his own life in 2010. It is a shock that reverberates - probably forever. I have no background in the science or the experiments or machines Robert loved and made, and I found his YouTube channel by mistake when I searched for “wind power”. He was entertaining, so very clever, and so very interesting. I am so glad to have watched him. Peace to you. Thank you. ☀️

author: @isoguy.
text:
Legacy to Robert
In quiet halls where shadowed thoughts abide,  
A voice once bright now silent in the tide,  
Robert, whose light was snuffed too soon,  
Left us in sorrow, stunned, and keen.  

Yet in his work, a legacy is sown—  
The seeds of truth in minds yet unknown.  
Through every formula, each careful page,  
His spirit lives, a beacon through the age.  

Though fate may steal the flame, it cannot quell  
The fire he kindled in the hearts of all.  
His family, dear, take heart and stand,  
For Robert’s science shall forever span.  

No death can dim the stars he sought to know—  
His name shall echo where his wisdom grew.
Rest In Peace Robert

ISO Guy

author: @LordGuardian
text:
I am so sorry for your loss.

These news...they are beyond heartbreaking.
Robert was a man of such positive and vibrating energy. I can´t imagine that anyone here suspected that anything was wrong with Robert. 

In all his videos Robert went about with that trademark lust of tinkering, trying, teaching and thrilled energy of his. 
I remeber one video where he was trying on a new kind of wind turbine, which worked very well and with a vivid smile and a laugh filled to the brink with vim Robert declared the experiment a success, followed by an explanation about why it worked.
And this is how I will choose to remeber Robert. Like an honourable and learned technology teacher extraordinaire.

Please leave the channel up.

I enjoyed his presentations very much! They were enormously didactic with the basic spirit of "you can do it yourself, just continue the road I paved for you". 
He was one of the masters in keeping not just mine, but many's  interest in this too vast a collection of personal contributions and reports, keeping focus on the essentials, even being just physically, but with a seed of hope to change the world "trickle by trickle", if I may say so. 
I will miss his enthusiastic presentations and will remember him.
He was a gentleman all along the way.
Robert will live forever in our memories.
Let his channel live forever!

author: @haveakek
text:
your actions have greatly undermined his efforts. you're his own brother supposedly, yet its rather clear you didn't know/care for him well enough. he didn't want a memorial or anything. anyone paying attention to him for a long period of time knows he was struggling, and probably already suspected this to a degree. the lack of replies to comments on his last video was the thing that solidified it for me.

out of respect for him many people, myself included, ignored the signs and just tried to let him end things on a positive note. you instead decided to ignore his wishes and do this. its possible that he somehow otherwise may have told/implied for you to do something like this but at least from my perspective this is in bad taste. even if its for charity asking people for money here is just infuriating.

if you didn't mean for this and instead were actually just trying to do good for him please take this video down. if people are really curious about what happened to him they can find out.

this channel is his legacy on its own; you can tell how much he cared about it. if you want him to be remembered archive and preserve this channel. let his last video be his last video.

author: @yougeo
text:
Honestly it's the weirdest thing but I really feel like I lost a friend. I had a few chances to email with him several years ago and just was always touched by his enthusiasm and his just genuine joy in informing everybody in his explorations and making us all a part of them. He was Way beyond a podcaster. He invited us into his inner sanctuary and enjoyed having us there. Everyone has their reasons and no one has a right to say that someone doesn't have their own best decision in mind when they do something like this but I do wish he had stuck around a little longer. And I want to wish you his brother and his family and those co-workers he had in the lab with him all the best. And don't feel rushed to work on a tribute video to him. If you feel like you have the energy to do it now then do it now or if you want to wait and do it when some time has passed and the shock has worn off then do that. His volume of videos speak for themselves so there is no pressure.

I think your choice of charities is the perfect one. And what you say is so true. Men do experience the burdens of life in very different ways and are expected to carry much of that privately. So it is a special charity that focuses on men's issues in that way.

while I don't judge Robert at all it is important for everybody to realize that every day is a gift. Just waking up and getting to see the Sun as a gift. So do not let the stresses of everyday life that really don't matter make you forget that just waking up and enjoying the outdoors and seeing the sunrise is a rare thing that you will not be able to experience forever and wow physical pain and deterioration probably is as good a reason as there is there is rarely a good reason to cut short your chance to see the next sunrise. So make sure you keep it all in perspective.

Those around us who have suffered from disease and passed away would give anything for one more sunrise and in a way we all cheapen their loss if we take it for granted and give it up voluntarily.

But I have to say Robert is one of the few people that I would trust who had probably the best perspective and I don't fault him at all for his choice although he will be missed.

replies.author: @tman250
replies.text: I've found Dr K's youtube channel useful for many things mind related, so I thought I'd share (search youtube for HealthyGamerGG). I've been on a health journey recently and have been incredibly surprised by how much can affect us mentally (even setting aside our thought patterns). Correcting imbalances in gut bacteria, correct supplementation (e.g. Vit. D, creatine etc.), exercise etc. all help to a very large degree. I know this firsthand because I've been in the middle of multiple crises involving several people. It's not simple or easy - but it is possible to overcome. If you can, try and research both ends of the spectrum - how to transform your mind and self (Dr. K's stuff will help there), and how to naturally improve your biochemistry. It's a journey, but it is worth it. One other thing that has helped me is being generous, having a spiritual practice and creating circumstances where I act better than my environment. Whatever you may feel now, there is always a potential for more, for better. You can not only carve out a place for yourself in the world, but create the conditions to thrive. You are your own blessing.

author: @m.h.english4773
text: My heart aches for Robert’s entire family. We had two suicides in our family 20 years ago. One spouse helped the other, then felt so guilty afterwards, she stopped eating and followed suit not even four months later. It literally tore our entire family apart. I was so upset, I wasn’t sure I would ever be happy again. I’m glad I was wrong about that, but there was so much grief to work through before getting to this point. The best thing Robert’s family can do for themselves right now is surround themselves with a group of people who have lots of LOVE.  They will make the most progress towards healing with that loving caring group than they will paying money to see trained professionals (which isn’t a bad idea either).  If any of Robert’s family is listening, WE LOVE YOU JUST AS MUCH AS WE LOVED ROBERT!!!!!! 😢

replies.author: @m.h.english4773
replies.text: I want to take a moment to ask that Robert’s YouTube channel remains so that others can watch. At the end of most of his videos he would remind the viewer not to forget to like and subscribe. One of the reasons I chose to do both is that I’ve had to fight to remain positive, and Robert’s videos gave HOPE to so many others like myself who needed new ideas and possibilities to start thinking differently in terms of formulating solutions to problems instead of resigning to simply to, “having to cope” with those problems. Robert have not only possible solutions to these problems, but most importantly he gave his viewers HOPE that present problems CAN BE SOLVED. And it’s hard not to love his infectious positivity. 😇

author: @steveguestart3377
text: A profound sorrow and grief for a gentle man in deep pain. An expression of thanks for Robert’s contribution to my personal happiness in what I understood to be a time of brave faced generosity to us all. In such a conflicted and difficult world, this is truly a loss to the part of humanity that wants for peace and good in our fellow man. Robert was an important person, contributing to this need through education, good humour and example. In his parting we can be none but buoyed in the sea of sorrow that his legacy in his works here will continue to inspire and calm.

author: @1coin1play
text:
My deepest condolences to Robert’s family and loved ones. He embodied the ideals of curiosity, independence, and thoughtful experimentation, qualities that inspired so many of us. Even in his final choices, there was a reflection of the efficiency and clarity that shaped his philosophy. Human minds are complex systems too, and his legacy of creativity, empowerment, and careful thought will continue to inspire those who learned from him.

I hope his content endures forever, as he foretold, continuing to inspire curiosity and self-reliance in all who discover it.

author: @K.P.Alexander
text:
Don't be sad that he's gone. Be glad that he was here.
That said, it's impossible not to feel a profound sense of loss. He was a true embodiment of curiosity, a reminder that the spirit of exploration lives not just in labs, but in garages and workshops everywhere. He inspired thousands to simply ask questions and pick up a soldering iron to find the answers. For that spark of inspiration, he will be deeply missed. My condolences to his family, friends, and all who followed his work.

author: @dereinzigwahreRichi
text:
He always spread such childlike and genuine joy about his creations in his videos, I really enjoyed seeing that and wished there was more people like him.
Also he was always a man of action not satisfied with what could be possible until he tried it out. As this man of action he chose to leave this world on his own terms.

Please keep his digital inheritance alive for us, it's an immeasurable treasure of knowledge and joy.

Perhaps you could also approach a service like Nebula and put up his content there, too. It's a service set up by creators and science communicators without restrictions of the YT algorithm, so it might be a safer backup space. It costs very little for viewers but offers great access to knowledge, I guess Robert would have liked this, if he didn't already knew about it. The little revenue created could also go to that foundation you're collecting for.

author: @flash001USA
text: I wasn't expecting to see this. Been a follower of this channel for many years. I did find his last video to be a bit odd but I had no idea that it would literally be his last video and I wish Robert Murray Smith peace and success wherever his new journey carries him and my condolences go out to his family. He always appeared to be very upbeat and excited with sharing his vast science and electrical knowledge with others on his channel. I'm a few years older than Robert was but I know what it's like to lose a long time spouse as I'm sure there are others that visit or subscribe to this channel have experienced loss of a loved one or a spouse too. For me it was like literally losing a piece of oneself and that was one of the hardest things in my life that I had to heal from and it was a struggle to get past it but for him to be experiencing health issues on top of his loss too, I can understand his mindset and what led him to the point of wishing to let go. God speed sir, you will be missed.

author: @noflaghumanist_13
text:
My most sincere and humble condolences.

As someone who worked in resource supporting men during tough times, I never get used to the harsh reality of losing a participant.

The most beautiful thing with this profession is that you are growing as a human being with every client.

On an even more personal level. I am myself fighting with important health issues since 25 years which forced me to stop working as a social worker at an early age, I know how rough it is to hold on to something when life seems pointless. Mental health becomes a daily challenge.

All this to say that I'm shocked by this news but I can understand the tough process behind the decision.

And 1even if I think myself about this option. I encourage anyone to seek help before making the move. Often it ends up being a definitive solution to a momentary issue.

author: @sB3rg
text:
I am sorry for your loss.  He was an incredible person. Robert made me realize that tinkering isn't just an idiosyncrasy but it can also a way of life.  Through his many videos, he made me see something in myself that I hadn't realized was so important.  He taught me that navigating the world with deep curiosity can be a meaningful way to express yourself, that curiously seeking endeavors can be an end in and of itself, and that the world is filled with amazing wonders if only one would stop to explore and to see.

His laughter always brought a smile to my face.  He was always so visibly delighted in his tinkering and that delight will surely carry on through his community.  He will be missed. Thank you for sharing the news of his passing.

author: @joeb9336
text: While I don't know Rob beyond his frequent videos, this has shocked and affected me very deeply.  I am so sorry that this has happened to Rob and his family.  I first watched his videos because they sparked my interests in engineering but subscribed to his channel because of his enthusiasm, delivery, smile and often laughter as he talked about his latest ideas.  He made me feel like there are other people in the world like me who are bursting to explore new ideas and he was able to share his ideas so beautifully.  For those from the UK of a certain age, I'm sure you'll understand when I say Robert was like Johnny Ball for my adulthood.   I very much appreciate you taking the time for making such a generous video Dave, thank you.  But most of all, thank you to Rob for sharing.

author: @daveshongkongchinachannel
text:
What a terrible thing to have to come on here and deliver this news. 
Most of what Robert did was well beyond my knowledge and skill level but I found his videos fascinating and marvelled at the wide range of topics he covered.
Although I didn’t read too much into the last video at the time, part of me felt something was not quite right and it all seemed so sudden to just end the channel like that. He even seemed to be branching out into new directions with his solar project. But in the end, it was obvious that he and Patty were soulmates and although most of us never have such a relationship, I can only compare it to the loss of a first love and just multiply that by a thousand! 
I personally believe the soul lives on and reincarnates. It’s a personal thing and others will have their own beliefs but for me it has helped me deal with the loss of people who touched my life in one way or another. And although I didn’t know Robert personally, he did once reply to one of my comments and for some reason I felt as if I did know him.

author: @gringo1723

Condolences. I would share that donating to Suicide Prevention seems to negate the importance of Robert choosing his own path.
His choice was his Individual and Personal right: to live and die as he pleased. Societies seem impossibly addicted to being 
Judgmental when ever a person takes their Life into control by choosing to end it. In Western Societies 'tis culturally enforced by 
the RELIGEOUS PROGRAMMING impressed upon us from earliest childhood. Respect this Man's choice. Rest well, Robert. ♾

author: @Sweetlyfe
text: I’m am very sorry to hear about Robert he was a brilliant Teacher.  I am 2yrs younger than him and I lost the love of my life 4.5yrs ago and it was a great struggle to continue on.  It only got better when I sought out grief counselling, but it was a good two years of each day trying to push through those thoughts.  I still miss her terribly but I only got through with the help of grief counselling, so please any other fella struggling please seek out help.  Sending heartfelt condolences to you and the rest of his loved one’s.

I hope his videos will remain and continue to inspire others for many years to come. I hope he has found peace and is together with Patti once again. He was a truly brilliant man who will be very sorely missed. RIP

author: @Reman1975

It somehow feels wrong to put a "like" on this video, but I suppose if it get's more eyes on the subject of men's mental health it's necessary. 😕

I'm sorry for your loss. He always came across as such a happy person in his videos, but now I think about it, when I'm in severe physical pain, and the prescription meds aren't helping, my sense of humour goes into overdrive, so you can't necessarily tell what's going on inside.

He'll be missed.

author: @DaNo1Animal
text:
Me & my Kate are so sad to learn this about Robert.
Even though just through a screen, I felt a connection with Robert somehow, to me Robert felt like a trusted family member sharing his knowledge & discoveries.
I particularly loved his passion for building & experimenting which shone through when he would laugh like a mad scientist on achieving a positive result! I've always loved that.
I will continue to replay his videos in remembrance.
He truly was an inspiration & will be sorely missed!
As his Brother, this time must be extremely hard & painful for you, especially as you are honourable & brave enough to share this message through Roberts platform for the sake of informing those like me who loved spending time with him, even if only on a screen. Thank you so much for your compassion!
With saddened hearts we wish you peace & healing for your pain.
With love,
Kate n da Animal.

I’m so very saddened by this news. My deepest condolences to all the family, it is such a huge loss for the community he’s built but so much more for all of you. I feel this will take some time for us all to process. Thank you so much for sharing this here.

author: @Theracles
text:
Typing through the tears here!
There is nothing I can say about what Robert meant to me that hasn't already been said.
I'm so terribly sorry that he bore so much pain since the death of his beloved Patti, we all knew that he missed her profoundly, but never the depth of that loss.
Robert's videos meant a great deal to me and as an engineer myself, I marvelled at his magnificent teaching ability.
Thankyou for letting everyone know, it must have been a difficult video to make and in true "RMS" style, you did it well!
The Link to CALM is such a lovely and fitting tribute to a magnificent man.
My deepest condolences to you and the wider family, you are in my thoughts. ❤

author: @orek1540
text: I never met Robert, but I really liked him. He felt like a personal friend, and improved my own mental health while his own suffered. He was genuine, talented, knowledgeable and friendly. He made the inaccessible accessible. He made subjects i had found boring, interesting. He made me do things myself that i would have paid for previously, saving me money. In my opinion, he was an example of what a man should look like in this ridiculous world. In Robert's own style of getting into the practicalities i wanted to try and work out the length of the task you have set yourself of watching all of his videos and picking out the best bits. Robert made 2440 videos with a very approximate average run length of 10 minutes. Thats a minimum of 24,400 minutes of footage or 10 weeks of continual watching @ 8 hours a day. Please let his community know if you need assistance with this task in any way.

author: @mattstroker
text: I am absolutely saddened by this news. I don't cry quickly but in this case I must admit that some tears are very near. I can certainly imagine how deep the loss of a magnificent woman can feel. I had to lose one too. And when you're always in pain, not just mentally and emotionally but also physically and especially when you are smart and creative, and you look out into this current world and get the feeling like you've woken up in the Idiocracy universe every day... well, it can make sense to some to want to end it all. My request to everyone at that point would be to not do it and push past the pain. Yet again. Till the grim reaper comes himself. Without your assistance. Please. Cuz unless you're currently on fire or terminally ill, in time, things do often really get better. Stay strong. Be that warrior that won't back down. Not even for yourself.

mit großem Bedauern habe ich von dem Tod Deiner geliebten Frau erfahren. Und heute, am 08.Oktober 2025 von Deiner Entscheidung.

Du warst ein großartiger YouTuber, von dem ich sehr viel gelernt habe. Oft genug hast Du selbst über Dich gelacht, was Deine menschliche Größe zeigte.
Ruhe in Frieden, Robert. Dein Lebenswerk bleibt für mich unvergesslich.
Mit tiefem Mitgefühl für Deine Familie
Scan Train

author: @timofthomas
text: Oh Dave.     I am so sorry to hear this news, I know how much he meant to us as his viewers, and i know how much family means to us, it must be incredibly hard for all of you just now. Thank you for letting us all know especially as it must still be very raw. Please share our Love with the rest of the family. Keep talking to each other, there are so many good things to remember about Robert, I am sure even before he started to record and you have a wealth of things to smile and remember. We knew he took Patti's departure very hard and these things do not just go quietly from our hearts no matter what happens on the surface. Stay strong for each other and do not be afraid to tell each other when you need support. <Hugs>

author: @ThePostApocalypticInventor
text: I'm heartbroken. I had a long private zoom talk with Robert when I prepared my last trip to the UK in 2022. He had invited me to his house and had even prepared a surprise for me. In the end I couldn't come because I got ill on my trip and didn't want to infect him. I was really sorry about it, but stayed in contact with him. Last year I told him that I might be back to the UK soon and he told me that I would be welcome at any time. Now that second chance has passed and I'm really sad about this. I missed the chance to meet a great man. May he rest in peace.

We will always carry that part of him with us.  God Bless.

author: @julieturner8158
text:
I am no scientist, but became inspired by Robert during lockdown. I loved his video about making a little heater using tin cans and had a go myself - the first science project I have ever done voluntarily. I was uplifted by his laughter and enthusiasm. I wish that I had been taught science by someone like him.
I was gutted to watch your video yesterday evening, but after sleeping on it find that I have great respect for his choice. 
It seems to be in character that he made his final video warning us that there would be no more - very dignified I thought.

My heart goes out to your family, what a loss. But his enthusiasm and laughter will certainly remain with me and I hope it does for you too. x

Sending my deepest condolences to all his family, friends and followers.

Dave, my thoughts are with you as you navigate through what must be some very challenging times.

author: @Anon-mk4ms
text:
I would ask all the people here, and especially the men, if you are suffering with mental health problems then please find someone to talk to, even if the person is not a health professional  it is better to talk things out than bottle them up. I've lost two good friends in the last five years and I wish they had talked to me or someone (anyone) else rather than take their own lives. If you find yourself "making plans" on how to exit, then it's urgent you find help, don't delay, go find that friendly ear.

RIP Robert Murray-Smith (1963 - 2025).

author: @TheCyberSalvager
text:
I'm absolutely shocked to see this. What I saw of Robert's video content was really fascinating and well presented, but recently  I have been trying to grow my own channel and got caught in the "Quagmire" of social media so only managed to dip in and out of his videos occasionally.
Having had issues with poor mental health myself (Depression since my late teens and anxiety that really got going when my marriage broke down about 15 years ago, as well as dealing with the aftermath of it - I won't go into details about that here) I can understand what it means to say "I'm fine" when deep down I was in a bad place. I think there is not only a stigma attached to men's mental health, but personally I have experienced a lack of empathy whenever I have dared to open up about things. Here's hoping that this will end for all of us in the same situation.
Please accept my deepest condolences. Alan.

Robs encouragement really helped me get a lot further in chemistry than i ever would. I remember doing a group thing where we made nano particles via double displacment reaction and still to this days i tell people that i know how to do it and learnt in the back shed and they laugh not realising that i am serious.I wish i had of been able to sit down with him and had a cup of tea and had a good chat with him about stuff. I know a lot of us will be hurting from this and my heart goes out us all. He really was the greatest teacher i ever knew.
 R.I.P RMS

author: @weedfreer
text:
Oh man.

I thought the video calling it a day was suspiciously short and lacking in proper context… but, for it to have been on the back of something so tragic was certainly not on my RADAR!

oh my, this is bringing back memories of a young lad who did the same at work a few years back.  Not good.

They also supported C.A.L.M. at his funeral.

I first became aware of C.A.L.M. at Snowbombing 2012.

They were selling a CD compilation they’d put together of largely Manchester based bands to raise money … not sure whether they’re still doing such a thing, but, listening to it it was really quite good.

Also, they were the first to highlight the fact that the biggest killer of men, even back then, between 16 and 45 is suicide.

That’s truly shocking, and, I believe the founder of the charity found this out for themself after their brother did the same.

C’mon lads. We’re better than this.  If you’re going through it, let someone, anyone, know.

Don’t allow yourself to become another number.

Reach out to one of these wonderful charities at the very least, make contact, give yourself purpose once again.

Don’t do something like this.

RIP Rob, such a shame.

😥

Secondly I'll be very sad to not hear Rob again, some years back my grandfather died, he was a sweet beautiful man who loved the world, he was a coal miner from Durham, he married my grandmother from there.
My grandfather was distinctly different looking and voice but like rob he had a jolly nature and a sparkle in his eye, I adored that about rob, it was a comforting abstract tether to my grandpa so losing rob is particularly saddening, in my heart is a space of gratefulness towards rob, I've lost an elder I had profound respect for.

author: @brendancaulfield970
text:
Dave, I'm dreadfully sorry to hear this. My heart breaks for you and your family. Thank you for taking the time to speak to us. Huge, huge respect to you and your family for the way you have handled this - there was absolutely no onus on you to do this for all of us. 

When Rob ended the channel, I though it was odd, and I felt a bit guilty because I had not given his videos the attention they so richly deserve. 

His output was phenomenal, his work ethic obvious and titanic, and his passion, joy and creativity in the work were infectious. 

In spite of my less than dedicated following of the channel, I had noticed and wondered about the change in his demeanor - the gleeful chuckling as his mind was tickled by engineering marvels reduced hugely. 

I hope in some way he can be with Patti now, and I hope that idea can bring your family and his friends some solace. 

I do think you should leave the channel up, at the very least as a fantastic resource of engineering and ingenuity - and any profits from its continued operation should be viewed as a bequeathal from Rob. 

I wish I knew him and gave him more of my time in life, I'm going to be reviewing the channel and trying many of the experiments and contraptions he brought to us. 

RIP Rob, you legend.

text: Thank you for letting us know what happened. You're very strong for being able to do this. I feel like my heart has died. Probably a lot of people were very surprised that he suddenly stopped making videos. So many people, including myself, absolutely loved Rob for the innovations he presented, his jovial yet serious manner, the enthusiasm and joy he brought to his work. He was, and is, a tremendous inspiration to myself and so many others. I will always remember Rob and will always be grateful not just for what I learned from him, but for him opening the door to an entirely new world that I didn't even know was there. The body of work he leaves us is truly a gift to the creative spirit of people everywhere. Please, please do keep the channel online. I'm sure if funding was needed, you could easily gather the necessary donations in no time. Thank you, Rob. We'll never forget you. Love always to our departed genius.

author: @BlackDidThis
text:
Just try rewatching his last recorded video after hearing these news...
One should not pass by without appreciating how he has isolated us all from his misery. Much like a parent to their children... You can notice his struggle only when you realize how much he has avoided them to slip past. And in no other way.
I hope they have found each other gain, as it meant so much for him.
I know so few that would attempt at what his brother has chosen: viewing the so many videos where his larger than life character illuminated the whole subject.
Not being as close he is. It hurts me. One can not fathom what he is going to endure...
And my hats off to him trying to honour the memory of his loved ones' years of dedicated work.
No one of my family, save from maybe my son; would go through such a task.

May the beautiful man find rest where he has chosen to be.

My sympathy to all who held him dear but had to let go.

author: @atrumluminarium
text:
Wow I don't know what to say, I'm actually tearing up in writing this 😢

First of all my sincere condolences for your loss especially how tragic the circumstances were. I wish you guys in the family all the strength in dealing with what is probably a very difficult time. 

I would also like to thank you for letting us know. I think a lot of people may share my experience, in that Rob was like an uncle I had never met to me. I would always put on his videos whenever I'd get home from university (now work) to see what he's been tinkering with, and whenever I was trying to solve a problem I would always come across one of his past videos providing a solution. His curiosity and passion for things were very infectious and I will admit made some tough times in my life bearable. 

I don't want to disrespect your wishes should you decide otherwise, but I think keeping the channel up might be a very useful tool both for people who love tinkering and for people who might need something in their life to be passionate about. 

It's such a shame he didn't get to read all the wonderful comments the community left on his farewell video. Robert truly changed a lot of people's lives.

author: @aceadman
text:
It’s often thought of as a desperate act by a damaged or misfunctioning mind. But I have always supported it as a rational choice, in many cases. And I imagine that he probably felt something similar. But knowing that someone made a choice that was “good” for them, doesn’t lessen the pain for those left behind, who will miss their presence badly. This was very hard for me to take in, because Robert seemed so vibrant and engaged in productive thought. We never know ALL that’s going on in the minds of our fellow comrades, and many of their choices are hard to accept. I will miss his voice. 

Thank you for sharing this information, and your grief with us. I, for one, appreciate it. My condolences on your loss and may good memories prevail for you.

author: @NykedDave
text: Oh no, Dave! I emailed him the day after the video. Robert was an absolute stalwart, and an inspirational inventor. I was hoping to visit him when coming to the UK in a year, but alas, that is not to be. I've followed Robert since the early days of his tinkering online with ultracapacitors. I collected dozens of cuttlefish from the beach and fried them in a home-made boiler to make one of his ultracapacitor designs. What a legend! He was the ultimate DIY-man and super-tinkerer. So knowledgeable and he shared his knowledge and play so unstintingly. There's something even more beautiful that he decided to follow Patsy before becoming a burden on others. He was brave as well as innovative. Much love to his family and you, Dave.

author: @sailorstu
text:
I am sorry about your loss Russ 😢

Unfortunately I know first hand what living with pain is like.
Plus what living with depression is like.

For everyone who reads this.
If you are living with depression, there is help out there.
You just have to ask your doctor for it.

I have probably been living with depression for almost 50 years.
Like Robert, I was pretty good at hiding it.

Still being Functional made it impossible for even trained medical staff to realize I was having issues until I literally broke down in tears in the doctors office about 8 years ago.

I now have counseling close at hand when I need it.
Antidepressants help me, although they don't work for everyone.
Even with that help I still go through some relapses. 

To be honest, if it wasn't for this help.
I would be in another place, tinkering on some devices with Robert at this very moment.

author: @patrickmark3678
text: Thanks so much for sharing this. When I discovered Robert on you tube it felt like I had finally found my lost lab partner. as a research scientist he made me feel that it was a great idea to wander off the beaten track and search for solutions in ways that were fun. He helped me feel that some of my crazy ides were really useful and that helped me on my way to several discoveries that have changed my work and took me to places that I previously was uncomfortable in going. Now working with my 2 young daughters in the lab I realized how much effect he had on my interactions with them. Even my 100 year old father watched some of his videos and enjoyed the ways that Robert searched for solutions. He has touched so many people in wonderful ways. We love you Robert. You will always be with us.

author: @theGreaterAwareness
text: Thank you Robert, for your videos - many of which I found very interesting and enjoyable to watch. The most important thing that one can do, is to be thankful for all things, and to forgive all things. In my 15 years of near-death experiences due to my own medical condition, I have explored much of the structure of existence - consciousness. The first time it happened, I learned that one cannot lose anything, as one is already everything. I began to practice thankfulness and forgiveness for those I disagree with the most, as they are one's counterbalance. Thankfulness is connection, resent is disconnection. Despite the way in which Robert passed, he is already forgiven, as I have been to a place beyond linear and non-linear time, a place where all have already forgiven all. One has but to remember the frequency - and the frequency is thankfulness. Forgiveness is what removes any selfish resistance that would reduce the flow of connections.

text: Condolences to you all. What a tragic thing to happen. I suspected Robert was going through terrible anguish as soon as he gave us the news of his wife's passing. The saddest thing is that even though many men struggle alone, it is often a choice because there's no solution to the fact that someone so dear to us is not there anymore, and nobody can take that agony away. Thank you Dave, I hope you and your family, as well as Roberts close friends, find comfort and peace as time passes. X

author: @oOMonkeyMagicOo
text:
I’m deeply shocked and saddened by this news. I never owned a 3D printer, and I never actually attempted any of the projects Robert demonstrated, but I watched every video simply because of his enthusiasm and the clarity with which he shared his knowledge. He had that rare gift of making complex ideas fascinating and accessible, the kind of presenter who could spark curiosity about things you never thought you’d be interested in.

Robert’s passion and kindness came through in every word he spoke. Even through a screen, he made learning feel personal and inspiring. The world has lost a brilliant teacher and a generous soul, and he will be truly missed. My deepest condolences to his family and all who loved him.

Wow.
Just. Wow.

I knew we were about the same age. I didn't realize he was just 10 months older. I suspect much of his audience were us Old Farts. We like to discover and learn. I have several of his small heaters made up and ready for power outages. I also have a bag of gelatin for clearing used fry grease, should I need it for fuel.

Thank you for letting us know what happened and his manner of death. I never met him, never even talked to him, I live in America, but he always seemed like one of my long distance Internet friends. The guy who always knew how to solve a problem. I will miss him.

text: The world lost a brilliant man. Not only a brilliant mind but a man with the heart to share his ideas with the world. I'll never forget the impact you had on my life. Your depth of knowledge and creativity was so remarkably inspiring. Rest in peace and may your ashes be scattered to the wind that you pondered so deeply.

We are faced with ENORMOUS problems, nowadays, and far too many people are shirking their duties in keeping dissolution, madness, and evil at bay. People like Robert withdraw because they think (often correctly) that there's no point in talking about it, because the rest of you don't have what it takes to actually solve any problems. I do know I struggle with my own MDD & I have not much outside of my abusive parents, if my health started failing it would be something I know I would struggle with more than I already do.

If it helps, my mind's perspective is that I'd rather have the choice than live not knowing anything, being able to do anything, & so on. There is something to be said about it, getting to a point & being afraid & it becoming that overwhelming. I liked his videos, when I could catch them. This hits hard, but no not that way in where you'll need to worry about me. 

I'm going to miss him & his smile. I hope he gets the recognition he deserves like everyone else does that deserves it.

author: @melkel2010
text:
Please accept my deepest sympathies. Robert was a wonderful teacher and inventor, and for sharing his knowledge so freely I would call him a philanthropist. I will miss him terribly, o my gosh. I only wished I was more like, I wished I had his talent and genius. 

I was widowed at 38 and the pain was sharp and frightening. It was months before I first laughed again and that pain striking sharp but the healing then began. However, I wondered the entire time how anyone in their senior years survive that pain, and indeed, its not unheard of to have one follow the other in brief succession. 

Yes, if anyone asked me how am I, I also answered, "I'm ok". There's no way to articulate the truth. There's no way to approach it. If anyone knows how it would be helpful if they start up. When no one was around I was a limp heap. As soon as someone came to the door I snapped up and was perky, like nothing was awry. I wasn't wearing a mask, it wore me. 

I hope anyone who is close to someone who is supporting a widower or survivor can take this and use it to try to learn to be more effective. When you were the rock, how can anyone be your rock. What does a rock need? I still don't know. 

I survived because the pain wasn't enough to naturally take me, although I thought it would, and in those times I didn't seek ER. I wanted to go in part and in the other part I didn't want to go into services and hear there was nothing wrong it was just the effects of mourning.

I survived for my children's sakes. I didn't want them to experience the loss of the other parent. Enough was enough for them, and they were young already, one hadn't even graduated high-school yet.

I am so sorry. He meant so much to a large community of tinkerers and dreamers, convention defyers and history buffs. Rest ye in peace and thank you for all you gave us, dear Robert Murray-Smith. You are loved and will be missed.

author: @benmcreynolds8581
text:
Robert will forever be an impactful individual, a profound individual. He had that rare kind of intelligence, that intelligence that could reach out & connect to people of all levels. He made learning Fun & interesting & that can have a profound affect on someone. It can make someone feel like Life is full of curiosity. Which can profoundly improve somebody's Life. 
As his brother, I just want to thank you for having the strength & bravery for being able to make this video. On top of that, I have so much respect for you that you were even willing to be honest with us about what had occurred. This level of honesty is the exact kind of thing that does & will make a positive impact on these kinds of difficult situations. I can't even express how grateful I am that you were willing to do this video & that your even willing to try to put together a little compilation video of some of your favorite moments of your brother's videos. 
I am deeply grateful. It was such a pleasure to have stumbled upon your brother's channel quite a while ago & then his videos became this really nice regular thing interwoven into my life & who knows how many countless others lives. 
Your impact, Robert's impact will ripple forever. He was a profound educator & he created a vast library of educational videos that will forever be timeless 💜 
Thank you

Hopefully, the community can come together to honor him in the best way ppssible, by making it possible for everyone to access that hidden knowledge so that we can build upon it further. author: @patricksmith4424
text: So sorry for you and your families loss and thanks so much for posting this video. Me and many others are totally shocked by this news. Robert was a man who exemplified life and positivity and its hard to believe he is still not around. His legacy will be that he made the best, most well researched, honest, educational videos on experimental alternative technology there is. His were head and shoulders above the rest. All this was intentionally achieved with just a home workshop and often recycled materials. Basically saying anybody can do what I am doing. There were quite a few red flags in his last video, he said it was "getting boring", no it wasn't. Something was not adding up and i strongly suspected a health issue, although i did not know about his wife's passing. RIP Robert you will be terribly missed.

author: @AaronHarper
text:
Thank you for letting us know so quickly.  To put out a video is no small feat, and even monumental while still so emotionally raw.  While I am deeply saddened by the loss, I fully understand his reasons and support his decision. 

Rob will be missed by all of us in the YouTube community he created, but those feelings must be insignificant compared yours and the rest of his family.  Please accept the love and hugs we all share as your own. If you need anything, please let it be known. If you wish to talk, PM us.  We all owe Rob, each in our own way, and would not hesitate to help in any way needed. 

In my case, Rob and I researched a few things together, and he is uniquely responsible for my own PhD.  I will echo the plea to leave his work up, as it is his legacy, and in most cases the only remembrance we have of him.

Our best wishes and hugs to all,

Dr. Aaron Harper

I hope you leave his work here for all the curious minds that come along later, looking to understand something that he made just the right explanation of.

author: @danherring5676
text: He was without doubt among the very best science educators I’ve ever encountered. His enthusiasm was obvious, his knowledge thorough, and his explanations clear. On top of all that, he was always able to engineer a practical project that perfectly illustrated the theories involved in the lesson, and delivered it with top notch presentational skill. Truly a gentleman and a scholar. Having an instructional video to create a useful project is always wonderful, but a full explanation of the why to accompany the how makes it a complete lesson. He was the total package, and I’ve always been curious about his training and experiences that made him such a great educator. We need more channels like this, he was a one of a kind. I had a weird feeling when I watched that last video of his. It was tough to hear he was signing off, but I had no idea it would be permanent. I’m so sorry to hear about his depression and that he succumbed to the darkness inside. You will be missed, Professor.

author: @oliverrafelsberger5554
text:
Oh, what sad news! Robert was such an inspiring person. I was always amazed at how quickly he could come up with all these ideas and solutions. Such a great, talented, and generous man!
On the other hand, I understand that Robert didn't want to experience his own decline while burdened by illness. For me, even though it may sound strange, it's also a sign of greatness when a person recognizes for themselves when it's time to go. It doesn't have to be out of despair; it can also be connected to a great deal of gratitude, awareness, and mental clarity.
Thank you for the message, even though it makes me very sad. Robert has enriched my life greatly.

All the best Robert, the way you saw and understood the universe was visible in your every word and facial expression and we'll always be in your debt for sharing with us.

To his family, friends and strangers, I wish you comfort and healing, I'm sure we feel a very deep loss, losing someone like Robert. I do hope Robert finally has peace, in what must have been a dreadful time in his life. Rest in everlasting peace Robert 😔😥🙏

I am thankful for the time we got with him, and pray that his videos will stay up, as there are so many projects I have been wanting to replicate, so much information to learn from him, and so much comfort in his mere existence, to still be had.
Lots of love from Norway, in your moments of grief.

author: @anysailer
text: Dear Dave, heartfelt condolences to you and Roberts family and friends.  I am so sorry for your loss.  I live in South Africa, am only a few years younger than Robert and only knew him from his channel which I watched regularly, and he became as much of a friend to me as anyone on a YouTube channel could be, so I feel no shame in admitting that I shed tears at this very sad news.  I, like many others, I am sure, learnt so much from him over the years, I even recommended that many of my friends and colleagues watch the excellent content that he produced, which was often extremely relevant to anyone interested in and in particular working in the engineering and scientific fields.  He was and shall always be remembered as a superb and gifted teacher.  In some of his videos he would become quite philosophical, revealing a deep and gentle sole, yet I doubt any of us knew of his personal struggle and anguish.  I am sure that I am not the only one who wishes to have known him better.  Rest in peace Robert, you shall always be fondly remembered.  Please, as a tribute to his life and work, ensure that his extensive body of work is somehow retained and kept available on the YouTube community.

author: @theneuroverse1210
text:
No way!!!! :(

My condolences to you all. It's a very difficult situation to deal with. I have been suicidal myself and currently am. I've also known people who have so understand it from both sides of the fence. There will be many who will judge. Many who will falsely accuse him of being selfish. It is NOT selfish and it's sickening that people will make these accusations and I hope that you all have the strength to get through them and through this difficult time.

I personally don't believe that we die per se, but we're all made of energy and energy changes from one form to another and certainly the videos and legacy the man left will forever live on and certainly through his infectious love of discovery and education inspired many others I'm sure, that he will certainly live on.

My thoughts are with you all and wish you peace and light.

author: @tomrock6134
text: Dave you owe us nothing. And I'm so sorry for your loss. I can't imagine how hard that video was to make, and I'm so grateful that you did. I'm 44 with 3 young kids, and I know it might sound silly - it's just YouTube - but your brother was a source of inspiration and optimism for me when I've had some dark times. He ignited a passion in me for tinkering with mechanical things, a passion I share with and enjoy with my boys. So in a small way the echos of your brother live on in the joy I have with my kids. I didn't know Robert,  but I'm profoundly sad that such a generous man felt so alone that this was the path he ended on. This isn't one of those things where I will have forgotten about it by next week, its going to be with me for a long long time. Regards, Tom

author: @rossphilpot7080
text:
I only found this channel last year. It was on a list of 'top 10 3d printing YouTube channels.' 

When I started watching Robert I was really amazed at his obvious delight at some of the gadgets he made. The almost childlike wonder and joy was infectious and I shared his enthusiasm with my kids and my friends.

I did see the picture of Patti on the wall behind him in almost every video and, as someone who has lived with depression most of my life, I wondered how he was coping with such a fresh loss: I guessed that he had poured himself into his work.

I am deeply effected by Robert's passing and am filled with sorrow that such an intelligent and gifted educator has suffered so much in silence.

RIP. xo

author: @Lethgar_Smith
text:
I am not sad for Robert. I am not sad for myself. I feel empathy for those who will be affected by his passing. 
He lived a full and rewarding life. There is always a little more to do as long as you are naturally alive. We are free to choose to leave early but when we do we are missing out on something our soul had planned for us to learn. Otherwise our soul leaves the body at exactly the moment best suited for it. 
Just like when you choose to drop out of high school. It may seem like a good idea at the time but as soon as you pull that lever, you realize you are missing out on some things that were still on the horizon. 
Oh well, guess you'll go learn those lessons somewhere else. It's not that big a deal and the worst thing that happens is you may feel a sense of regret or a kind of, "Oops, I shouldnt have done that but, oh well. It's done now, nothing to do but keep moving forward."

See ya around, Robert!

I feel completely frozen hearing this, stuck staring at the video’s page. 

I found Rob’s channel only a few years ago when I was at the start of a pretty devastating divorce; finding his videos and enjoyment to provide knowledge to anyone that would listen helped me in so many ways, but it truly helped my mind to keep busy. I didn’t know him personally, but we exchanged a handful of emails, which mostly consisted of him patiently answering my questions and I became a comment moderator for him, though I wasn’t as attentive as I should have been. I will truly miss him and I know that the world will be lesser without him.

Thank you for sharing this and I am sorry for your loss.

author: @GabrielPettier
text:
Thanks for letting us know, I had missed the previous video, being busy in the last few weeks, and I'm shocked, as much as I'm sorry for you and his family, and for us all.

I only discovered his channel early this year, when I got into 3D printing, and inevitably stumbled on one of his numerous models, freely shared for people to play with and learn, and the videos that came with.

His passion for explaining, his benevolence, and his great laugh when seeing things work, really didn't leave space for doubting he was still enjoying life, even if yes, the hints about how important Patty was to him, were pretty obvious, I though, as you probably did, that he was going on, and would in time, find more happiness, as I'm sure she would have wanted.

I'm glad to learn that he didn't suffer, I have seldom doubt that he knew what he was doing, and left with the best possible understanding of what would happen, because he was certainly as smart as he was good a person.

And thank you for talking about the difficulty of sharing about feelings, and getting the help we need when things are not right, he's another victim of that terrible condition, I hope we learn to do better to ourselves.

With sincere condolences.

author: @margaretjohnson4413
text: Thank you for this video.  Losing someone is so difficult to bear and health does suffer as a consequence. Walking can help, so it must have been even more unbearable to find he could not manage that. Please take care of yourselves, take extra vitamin D over the darker days of the year it does help with depression that comes from the shorter days and low light levels. It also helps us resist infection at a time when we are vulnerable, at a time of great loss and sorrow we need every little bit of help we can get.  Please accept our deepest sympathies for your loss.

text: I'm very sorry for your loss, but also  especially sorry he wasn't able to find peace following his wife's passing.
